content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 17, 1991

A. Kay Vinson
City of Barstow
220 East Mountain View Street
Barstow, CA 92311-9931

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-355

Dear Ms. Vinson:

We have received your request for advice under the conflict
of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

QUESTIONS

1. Does the City of Barstow's revised conflict of interest
code conform to the requirements of the Political Reform Act?

2. Under the revised code, must the Chief of Police disclose
as a business position his advisory board position with a local
bank?

CONCLUSIONS

1. The conflict of interest code does not appear to be
complete in that it fails to set out the standard provisions
required by Government Code Section 87302 or, in the alternative,
fails to incorporate Commission Regulation 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18730.

2. The terms of the conflict of interest code do not require
the Chief of Police to disclose his advisory board position with a
local bank.

___________
1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

September 17, 1991
Page Two


FACTS

In May of 1991, the Chief of Police for the City of Barstow
wrote to the Commission asking if he was required to disclose his
position on an advisory board to a local bank as a business
position on his statement of economic interests. He was advised
that since the city's conflict of interest code at that time did
not require the disclosure of business positions, such disclosure
was unnecessary. (Sessions Advice Letter, No. I-91-221.)

In your July 18, 1991 letter you indicated that the City of
Barstow took action on July 15, 1991 to revise the conflict of
interest code. It now requires the Chief of Police to disclose
investments and business positions in business entities and income
from sources which provide services, supplies, materials,
machinery or equipment of the type utilized by the chief's
department.

In your letter you further indicated that the particular
bank, of which the police chief is an advisory member, does not
contract or provide services to the police chief's department.
The City of Barstow does however utilize the services of the bank,
but the police chief's department independently does not.


DISCUSSION

The Political Reform Act requires every agency to adopt a
conflict of interest code. (Section 87300.) A conflict of
interest code must specifically enumerate each of the positions
within the agency which make or participate in the making of
decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any
financial interest. Further the code must set forth the type of
investments, business positions, income and real property which
must be reported by those designated employees. The types of
financial interests which must be reported are the types which the
designated employee can affect by his or her decision making.
(Section 87302.)

In addition to enumerating positions designated in the code
and providing corresponding disclosure categories, conflict of
interest codes must specifically set out all of the provisions
described in Section 87302(b). For example, Section 87302(b)
requires codes to specify such things as when the statements are
to be filed, what information is to be disclosed on those
statements, and what the reporting period would be for each
statement. Section 87302(b) provides that the code must also set
out the disqualification provisions as defined by Section 87103.
Rather than try to extract all of this information from the Act,
the Commission encourages governmental agencies to incorporate the
provisions of its Regulation 18730.

September 17, 1991
Page Three


While the conflict of interest code for the City of Barstow
designates the positions which participate in making decisions and
provides corresponding disclosure categories, the code fails to
set out the miscellaneous provisions required by Section 87302(b).
This can be rectified by either extracting all of that information
from the Act or by incorporating Commission Regulation 18730 as
the standard provisions of the code. Regulation 18730 sets out
all of the miscellaneous provisions required by Section 87302,
including the disqualification provisions of Section 87100. I
have enclosed a fact sheet which describes Regulation 18730 as
well as the procedure to incorporate its provisions into your
code.

The city did correct the disclosure categories of its code to
require the disclosure by its designated business positions. The
category for the Police Chief requires the disclosure of investments and business positions in business entities, and income from sources which provide services, supplies, materials, machinery or equipment of the type utilized by Police Chief's department. If the bank is not of the type to provide services of the type utilized by the department, then it is not necessary for the police chief to disclose his position with the bank.

We do have a concern which needs further review on your part.
Is the disclosure assigned to the positions listed as "Director of Personnel" through "First Operations Foreman" sufficient? We
noted that none of the positions are required to disclose
interests in real property. Can the Public Works Superintendent,
Wastewater Reclamation Superintendent or Transportation Officer
make or participate in decisions affecting real property? If so,
then you must require such disclosure from these positions as well
as any other positions which make decisions affecting real
property.

In your letter you stated that you were assuming that the
revised code would not affect any previously filed statements, but
would only affect statements filed following the July 15, 1991
amendment. That is correct. Designated employees do not need to
correct previously filed statements. The amended code became
effective on August 14, 1991, which was 30 days after adoption.
Only positions newly designated in the code must file an initial
statement 30 days from the effective date of the code. All
positions existing in the code when it was amended will continue
to file their annual statements.

September 17, 1991
Page Four


I trust this has answered your questions sufficiently. If
there is anything further you need, please feel free to call me at
(916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel


                                        By:  Jeanette E. Turvill
                                             Political Reform Consultant
                                             Legal Division

SH/JET/jt
Enclosure
